item.title: CPAPNG and Reset Delivery Unit collaborate on national financial reforms
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea has taken a major step in improving public sector financial governance with a new partnership between Certified Practicing Accountants PNG (CPAPNG) and the Reset Delivery Unit, aimed at supporting the PNG Reset @50 Agenda. CPAPNG President James P Gore said the Memorandum of Understanding highlights a shared commitment to transparency, accountability, and capacity building in gove
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea has taken a major step in improving public sector financial governance with a new partnership between Certified Practicing Accountants PNG (CPAPNG) and the Reset Delivery Unit, aimed at supporting the PNG Reset @50 Agenda.

CPAPNG President James P Gore said the Memorandum of Understanding highlights a shared commitment to transparency, accountability, and capacity building in government financial management.

The PNG Reset @50 Agenda, launched as the nation celebrates its 50th anniversary, sets a 20-year roadmap for sustainable development, good governance, and national unity.

Established in 1974, CPAPNG has nearly 10,000 members and will now assist in implementing, monitoring, and reporting on the Reset @50 Agenda.

Proposed amendments to the Accountants Act will integrate public sector accountants into CPAPNG, offering certification as Certified Public Sector Accountants and ongoing professional development.

The partnership also aims to advocate for policy reforms, strengthen governance, and build capacity across all levels of government.

The upcoming PNG Reset @50 Conference on May 14 will provide a platform for knowledge-sharing and dialogue between accounting professionals and government stakeholders.

CPAPNG has expressed gratitude to the Reset Delivery Unit and all partners for their collaborative efforts, reaffirming a commitment to promoting excellence, accountability, and a stronger, more prosperous PNG.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Kokopo MP assures Malapau residents land titles soon
item.description: KOKOPO MP Ereman ToBaining Jr has reassured residents of the Malapau plantation that their long-awaited land titles will soon be issued. Speaking at a meeting in Karavi village, he addressed residents from the four allotments that make up the plantation – Balanatam, Karavi, Vunamami and Ranguna.
item.content:encoded:
KOKOPO MP Ereman ToBaining Jr has reassured residents of the Malapau plantation that their long-awaited land titles will soon be issued.

Speaking at a meeting in Karavi village, he addressed residents from the four allotments that make up the plantation – Balanatam, Karavi, Vunamami and Ranguna.

He confirmed that survey work had been completed and the process of issuing individual land titles was nearing completion.

ToBaining said the land was previously acquired under an agricultural lease through former business groups, and the titles had to be forfeited before new individual titles could be issued.

“There were technical issues that delayed the process,” he said.

The MP also assisted block holders financially, contributing funds to help them pay the required K600 per block.

He reminded residents that the land falls within the proposed Kokopo city boundary expansion and urged them to secure their titles, saying land values would increase once the expansion takes effect.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bougainville frontline workers complete counselling training to tackle gender-based violence
item.description: FRONTLINE workers in the Autonomous Region of Bougainville are now better equipped to support survivors of violence after completing a specialised 10-day basic counselling training in Buka. The initiative, supported by the Australia–Papua New Guinea Law and Justice Partnership, brought together 30 participants, including safe house staff, health workers and community development officers.
item.content:encoded:
FRONTLINE workers in the Autonomous Region of Bougainville are now better equipped to support survivors of violence after completing a specialised 10-day basic counselling training in Buka.

The initiative, supported by the Australia–Papua New Guinea Law and Justice Partnership, brought together 30 participants, including safe house staff, health workers and community development officers.

The training aimed to strengthen their ability to respond to gender-based violence and sorcery accusation-related violence.

It was delivered by the PNG Counsellors Association through the National GBV Secretariat with funding from the Australian government.

Bougainville Minister for Community Development and Public Service Melvin Wilolopa said the training addressed an urgent need for practical counselling skills within communities.

“Here in Bougainville, our people face serious challenges, including family violence and sorcery-related attacks.

“Many survivors carry emotional wounds we cannot see,” Wilolopa said.

“Counsellors play a special role in helping them be heard and supported.

“This training gives the skills to do that, and we are grateful it has reached our region, where the need is real every day,” he said.

Participants learned basic counselling techniques and how to ensure support for survivors is safe, confidential and coordinated, while strengthening referral pathways with police, health services and community organisations.

The training forms part of a broader national effort under the PNG–Australia Partnership to strengthen counselling services across PNG.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ombudsman’s referral of Sir Gibbs ‘unprecedented’
item.description: CHIEF Justice Sir Gibbs Salika has been referred by the Ombudsman Commission to the acting Public Prosecutor, in what the Government has described as an “unprecedented territory”. Prime Minister James Marape, in a statement last night, described Sir Gibbs’ referral as an unprecedented matter involving one of the nation’s most important constitutional offices.
item.content:encoded:
CHIEF Justice Sir Gibbs Salika has been referred by the Ombudsman Commission to the acting Public Prosecutor, in what the Government has described as an “unprecedented territory”.

Prime Minister James Marape, in a statement last night, described Sir Gibbs’ referral as an unprecedented matter involving one of the nation’s most important constitutional offices.

“This is not a light matter. It is unprecedented territory for our country. The referral of the chief justice – the head of the judiciary – raises serious constitutional considerations,” Marape said.

Marape directed Chief Secretary Ivan Pomaleu to convene an urgent meeting with senior officials to review the matter and ensure that the integrity of the judiciary and constitutional processes was fully respected.

The meeting is to include Pomaleu, the Secretary for Justice and Attorney-General, the State Solicitor, and the Secretary for Judicial Services.

Justice Minister and Attorney-General Pila Niningi confirmed that Sir Gibbs was referred to the Acting Public Prosecutor but had not been charged with any allegation under the Leadership Code.

Niningi, also the chairman of the Judicial Legal Service Commission, assured the public and staff of the National Judicial Staff Services that the administrative operations and functions of the judiciary would continue as normal as possible.

“The Chief Justice of Papua New Guinea was not charged but only referred in a notice by the Ombudsman Commission to the Acting Public Prosecutor. There is no tribunal set up yet,” he said.

“The constitution process shall take its course and all the administrative operations and functions shall continue as usual.”

Marape said the Government respected the independence of constitutional offices, including the Ombudsman Commission, and would not interfere with their duties.

“I have no authority to compel the Ombudsman Commission or any constitutional office holder to carry out their duties. They must perform their functions independently, and we respect that.”

Niningi said when the office of the chief justice became vacant, the deputy chief justice or, if unavailable, the next most senior judge would become the acting chief justice.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bilums top group
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea maintained their top Group B ranking in the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers, defeating hosts and leadership contenders Fiji 1-0 at Govind Park in Ba yesterday. Next up is the semi-final against American Samoa in Auckland, New Zealand, next month.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea maintained their top Group B ranking in the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers, defeating hosts and leadership contenders Fiji 1-0 at Govind Park in Ba yesterday.

Next up is the semi-final against American Samoa in Auckland, New Zealand, next month.

The PNG Bilums rampaged through the Fijians in the second half for Meagen Gunemba to secure the winner in the 86th minute from a Mavis Singara cutback.

Both nations join hosts New Zealand and American Samoa next month in Round 3 of the qualifiers in Auckland .

PNG was the better side yesterday, although opportunities did present to the Fijians earlier on after the interval when Trina Davis forced Betty Sam into a save from close range. But, the bets were already rolling favouring the Bilums when, in the first half, a Gloria Laeli-Ramona Padio corner delivery missed the right corner post by inches.

Speaking post-match, coach Ericson Komeng acknowledged all team members “for the hard work that we’ve put in together”.

Komeng noted the tough situations faced by the girls “leaving their families and children because we’ve been in camp for so many weeks”.

“The victory today shows their sacrifice and hard work since day one.”

He said it was now back to recovery mode; letting the girls rest for a few days before starting preparations for the semi-final against American Samoa in New Zealand.

Komeng also extended his appreciation and that of the Bilums to all their supporters both in Fiji and back home in Papua New Guinea. qualifiers’ semi-final

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Big weekend for four US-based PNG athletes
item.description: It was a big weekend for four Papua New Guinea United States-based athletes as they competed in the Junior College National Indoor Championships at Washburn University in Topeka, Kansas. Star of the show was Scholastica Herman who broke PNG indoor records for both 600m (1min 33.83) and 1000m (2min 56.28) in the finals on Sunday (local time).
item.content:encoded:
It was a big weekend for four Papua New Guinea United States-based athletes as they competed in the Junior College National Indoor Championships at Washburn University in Topeka, Kansas.

Star of the show was Scholastica Herman who broke PNG indoor records for both 600m (1min 33.83) and 1000m (2min 56.28) in the finals on Sunday (local time).

Johnny Bai set a new indoor record for the 200m with a time of 21.63secs and ran a 48.54second split in a tense 4x400m relay where his New Mexico Junior College team were trying to overhaul competition leaders Barton County in the final event of the day.

The team fell just one point short and had to settle for second place.

Adolf Kauba was also in action, running the 600m but was unable to match his 2025 performance where he made the final with an outstanding run of 1min 17.56secs.

Joy Tieba was also at the championships, running in the 4x400m relay for her Neosho County team.

The athletes are now all looking forward to the outdoor track season that gets underway in two weeks time and will culminate in the National Championships which New Mexico JC is hosting from May 14-16.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PETE’S PUNCHLINE
item.description: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
item.content:encoded: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: ESP to host Unity Games
item.description: EAST Sepik will host the 9th Unity Games in 2028, with preparations set to begin following the signing of a host agreement with the PNG Sports Foundation in Wewak. The agreement was signed on Friday at Sir Michael Somare Stadium in the presence of provincial leaders, officials and members of the sporting community.
item.content:encoded:
EAST Sepik will host the 9th Unity Games in 2028, with preparations set to begin following the signing of a host agreement with the PNG Sports Foundation in Wewak.

The agreement was signed on Friday at Sir Michael Somare Stadium in the presence of provincial leaders, officials and members of the sporting community.

Sports Foundation chief executive officer Micah James said sport has the power to drive development and unite communities if properly managed.

“Sports deal with people. It can bring economic development if managed properly. If we work together, we can prepare facilities on time and deliver the best games in 2028,” James said.

Administrator Samson Torovi assured the East Sepik administration’s full commitment to ensuring the event is successfully hosted.

“We will work closely with all stakeholders to ensure preparations are carried out in time for the games,” Torovi said.

Meanwhile, Wewak MP Stanley Muts Samban said hosting the event would bring opportunities beyond sport.

He said the games “will help identify raw talents and strengthening unity among communities and people from everywhere”.

Athletes, officials and supporters from across the country would be expected for the games, bringing economic activity and renewed focus on sports development in Wewak and the wider East Sepik.

The event would also highlight the province’s readiness to host major national events.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: New road a dream come true for lower Jimi people
item.description: NEWLY-constructed feeder road in lower Jimi is a dream come true for the lower Jimi people and laugh at last. For too long, the lower Jimi people have endured suffering and occasionally become victims of roadblocks and rascal activities along the main Jimi road corridor between Karap and Sipil.
item.content:encoded:
NEWLY-constructed feeder road in lower Jimi is a dream come true for the lower Jimi people and laugh at last.

For too long, the lower Jimi people have endured suffering and occasionally become victims of roadblocks and rascal activities along the main Jimi road corridor between Karap and Sipil.

The ongoing painful experience, nightmares, ordeals, loss of valuables and attacks that has been happening on the main Jimi road in the past would be no more for the lower Jimi people once this alternative shortcut road is completed and open for use.

However, this new road came about as a result of combined effort of manpower from the area along with funding and logistics support from the Jimi Development Authority (DDA).

This road, once completed and open for use, will save travellers time as it is several kilometres fewer than travelling the main Jimi road to Banz and Mt Hagen.

Thanks to the hardworking locals for the time and effort put into the initial phase of construction, with different jobs being done and the use of different tools.

Well done and great effort by the lower Jimi people — your hard work and sweat for an alternative road will be fulfilled in a short while.

My word of “thank you” also extended to the DDA for the funding assistance and the supply of heavy machineries, dump trucks and other work that humans can’t do manually.

Once again, thank you the lower Jimi people and the DDA for your vision in building an alternative road and naming it “Pressure Road”.

An ideal name because it was through the pressure of rascal attacks, roadblocks and other eyesores encountered on the main Jimi road have pushed the lower Jimi people into taking the onus in building their alternative road with the help of the DDA.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Use energy for PNG first
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea must take full control of its energy resources and use them to serve its people first. Our oil, gas, hydro, solar, and geothermal resources are national assets.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea must take full control of its energy resources and use them to serve its people first.

Our oil, gas, hydro, solar, and geothermal resources are national assets.

They must strengthen our economy, lower the cost of living, and secure our future.

PNG must develop its own refinery through a transparent state-supported partnership. A portion of all crude oil production must be reserved for domestic processing before export. This will reduce import bills, create employment, and keep value within the country. Strategic fuel storage depots must also be built to protect the nation during global supply disruptions.

A fixed percentage of oil and gas revenues must be placed into a protected National Energy Security Fund.

This fund will finance long-term projects, including hydropower dams, provincial solar grids, geothermal exploration, and transmission networks. Rural communities must be prioritised so that every district has access to reliable electricity.

Government must introduce a clear fuel pricing mechanism that cushions citizens from sudden international price shocks. Regulatory bodies must be strengthened to prevent unfair pricing practices and ensure competition in the downstream market.

All energy projects must include minimum local employment and skills transfer requirements.

Technical institutes must be strengthened to train engineers, technicians, and energy managers. Scholarships in petroleum, renewable energy, and energy economics should be expanded.

All major energy agreements must be subject to parliamentary scrutiny. Annual public reporting and independent audits must be mandatory to protect national interest and prevent misuse of revenue.

Cabinet endorsement should be immediate, followed by parliamentary debate and adoption within six months.

A National Energy Taskforce reporting directly to the Prime Minister should coordinate execution.

Papua New Guinea cannot continue exporting raw wealth while importing expensive finished fuel.

This policy secures affordable energy, builds national capacity, and protects future generations.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Stoning of vehicles disturbing and bad
item.description: A DISTURBING incident occurred on yesterday, around 8am at Bodiam One, Six-Mile in Port Moresby. A group of drunken youths were seen stoning random passing vehicles after one of their friends allegedly received a knife wound from someone at Five-Mile.
item.content:encoded:
A DISTURBING incident occurred on yesterday, around 8am at Bodiam One, Six-Mile in Port Moresby.

A group of drunken youths were seen stoning random passing vehicles after one of their friends allegedly received a knife wound from someone at Five-Mile.

For nearly 30 minutes, these youths continued to throw stones at vehicles and bottles at innocent pedestrians who were simply passing through the area.

A police cruiser eventually arrived at the scene.

However, there appeared to be only one police officer in the vehicle, accompanied by more than five civilians.

The officer remained in the vehicle while the disturbance continued.

It is assumed that the officer may have contacted the nearby Six-Mile Police Station or other patrol units for assistance.

After some time, two white police 10-seater vehicles arrived.

The officers attended to the injured boy and then left the area.

What is concerning is that the other youths responsible for stoning vehicles and threatening public safety were not identified or apprehended.

Equally troubling is the lack of visible intervention from community leaders to address the behaviour of these individuals.

These youths remain in the area and may continue to pose a risk to motorists and pedestrians, particularly when they are intoxicated.

This incident raises serious questions about public safety, police response times, and community leadership in maintaining order.

Members of the public who had their vehicles damaged or were threatened deserve assurance that such lawless behaviour will be addressed.

I hope the relevant authorities and community leaders will take this matter seriously and ensure that steps are taken to prevent such incidents from happening again.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prioritise mental well being
item.description: MENTAL health is a critical issue that affects individuals, families, and communities in Papua New Guinea. It is time for us to take it seriously and prioritise our wellbeing.
item.content:encoded:
MENTAL health is a critical issue that affects individuals, families, and communities in Papua New Guinea.

It is time for us to take it seriously and prioritise our wellbeing. 

The recent loss of a young man in Port Moresby, who took his own life while his only sister was away studying in Australia, is a heartbreaking reminder of the devastating consequences of untreated mental health issues.

This tragedy highlights the need for us to break the stigma surrounding mental health and encourage open conversations.

Mental health is just as important as physical health, and seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness.

In PNG, we’re often encouraged to be tough and keep our emotions bottled up, but this approach can lead to more harm.

We need to create safe spaces for people to share their struggles and connect them with support services.

If you’re struggling, please reach out to someone you trust, a healthcare professional, or a support hotline.

Your life matters, and there are people who care.

Let’s work together to prioritise mental health and support each other in our communities.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: LLG president Eugene understands hardship
item.description: WITH trust in God, Eugene Kauken, now a businessman, left home in East Sepik for Port Moresby more than 10 years ago in search of opportunities. “My lowest point was when I didn’t get an offer (from any tertiary institution) after Grade 12. It did not sit well with me as I was a role model in school and a leader among my peers.”
item.content:encoded:
WITH trust in God, Eugene Kauken, now a businessman, left home in East Sepik for Port Moresby more than 10 years ago in search of opportunities.

“My lowest point was when I didn’t get an offer (from any tertiary institution) after Grade 12. It did not sit well with me as I was a role model in school and a leader among my peers.”

Eugene, 35, is from Malba in Yamel-Tamaui, Maprik, East Sepik. He is the second last in a family of seven, and is married with two children.

He attended Ulupu Primary School where he completed Grade One to Eight from 2001 to 2008, then grades nine to 12 at Maprik Secondary from 2009 to 2012. He was the Student Representative Council president in Grade 12.

“I worked hard to maintain good grades, and at the same time take part in school programmes. And during that time I gave my life to God.”

Also while in Grade 12, he wanted to withdraw from school because he did not want to be a burden to his parents who were subsistence farmers. His father declined his request.

“I sat for the Grade 12 examinations and unfortunately, did not get an offer from any tertiary institution. It made me feel bad because I was a leader in school, local church and the community.”

He returned to the village in 2013 and started looking for gold along the river.

“I collected five grams of gold and sold it for K3,500. I bought an airline ticket to Port Moresby.”

He used K2,000 to cover his expenses while looking for work. He got an offer from a security firm to be trained as a dog handler.

My lowest point was when I didn’t get an offer (from any tertiary institution) after Grade 12. It did not sit well with me as I was a role model in school and a leader among my peers.”

In 2015, he was posted to be in charge of security at the Australian High Commission residential units at Kone and Waigani. Also that year, Eugene met his wife Glenda and had a son.

 In 2017, he was sacked for continually being absent from work. So he decided to return to Maprik with his wife and son.

The reception he received from his relatives was not what he expected. Everything he tried to do to support his family was met with resistance.

“This was my own family, and yet I was out of place. I had to think of something fast to support my little family.”

He went back to the river to look for gold.

“I worked along the river and sold whatever I found and made K1,000.”

He started a gold-buying business in 2018.

“I went to the Maprik town council and found a gold scale. I started buying gold – K150 for 140 ounces. After six months, I had made a K20,000 profit.

“Whatever gold I was buying I sold to big clients in West Sepik.”

In 2019, he decided to return to the Oceania Bible College in Port Moresby.

When he returned in 2020 to Maprik, a businessman gave him K50,000 to buy gold and resell it at market price. He was paid a commission. Later he was given K100,000 for the same purpose. From his commission, he bought a vehicle.

“I was making deals with large businesses, winning their confidence and trust in how I handled sales and transactions. I now have three vehicles. I lent a bus to some of my boys to assist them and rented a vehicle to the provincial administration.”

His daily income from the transport service surpassed K5,000, although the roads are bad.

Eugene then decided to try his hand in politics.

“I ran for the LLG presidency and used the road as my strategy to get my voters in place and won by block vote securing 13 communities along the road.”

He is now the Yamel-Tamaui Rural Local Level Government president.

“I run a business which my wife handles. We have a second child. I am working on plans to establishing cooperatives, marketing opportunities and road infrastructure, which will be presented to the district authority soon.”

He has experienced the life of an ordinary villager, and knows the difficulties one faced.

“I saw the real pain as I was once at the bottom like everyone else. I know what the people are going through. As leaders we must serve the people’s needs.”

item.title: Kambi returns to help youths
item.description: AT Amoora Street in Hohola One, Port Moresby, a quiet yet significant transformation is taking place – driven not by government policy or outside intervention, but by Kambi Stanfiel Poraikali who once walked the same troubled path he is now trying to change. Father-of-one Kambi, 25, created the North-West Gladiators sports team to keep young men in the area busy and distract them from illega
item.content:encoded:
AT Amoora Street in Hohola One, Port Moresby, a quiet yet significant transformation is taking place – driven not by government policy or outside intervention, but by Kambi Stanfiel Poraikali who once walked the same troubled path he is now trying to change.

Father-of-one Kambi, 25, created the North-West Gladiators sports team to keep young men in the area busy and distract them from illegal activities.

He is a member of the Malipin tribe, sub-clan Mai and Lyein of Kemoumale village in Wabag, Enga but was born and raised in Port Moresby – in a polygamous family.

He describes his situation as “tough yet exciting.” His mother, the second wife of his father, gave birth to five children. Kambi is the youngest of the five.

His mother left the family when his father remarried. Kambi was only nine then. He has 12 siblings from his father’s other wives.

Without a stable consistent parental guidance, the streets inevitably became his mentor.

“Most of my life’s lessons came from raw experiences with my peers. I was taught by the streets to survive.”

Kambi was involved in substance abuse and alcohol consumption. He recalls using his lunch money to buy yeast and sugar to make homebrew. He experienced firsthand how young lives can easily spiral out of control.

The turning point came one day when his elder brother Malipin Poraikali sat him down and gave him a stern talk about the importance of school and life. It was the first time his brother had spoken to him in that way.

“My whole perspective changed. He made me realise that I was meant for something greater.”

Kambi completed primary school at Eki Vaki, attended De La Salle Catholic Secondary School, and later graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea with a Bachelor of Business Management in Banking and Finance degree.

He then worked as a volunteer for two years before securing his first formal job. That experience shaped his mindset about responsibility and self-discipline.

Most of my life’s lessons came from raw, unfiltered experiences with my peers. I was taught by the streets to survive.”

Today, he works as a merchant sales specialist with CreditBank PNG.

He watched as his peers fall deeper into substance abuse, and decided to do something to save them too. He formed a sports team for them to be involved in.

“My past is the reason I created the North-West Gladiators club team in my area to engage youths there. If I can change at least one life, I would be the happiest man alive.”

Through sports, which he calls the universal language, he began bringing young men together. What started as a small initiative has now evolved into a catalyst for change in the community.

For the first time in nearly 20 years, Hohola One residents celebrated the 2026 New Year peacefully – no burning of tires, no street fighting, no smashing of bottles.

The young men have also stopped snatching bags at the Hohola bus stop. Substance abuse has significantly decreased among them.

“I see myself in them. But during my time, there was no one to guide us like this.”

He is at work from 8am to 5pm. Evenings are dedicated to family, and overseeing the youth group through delegated leaders and WhatsApp coordination.

Managing work, family, and community leadership is not easy. His wife sometimes questions the sacrifice he is making. But for Kambi, it is something personal.

“I’m grateful I went through everything young. Now I can never again be shaken or swayed by those things.”

He plans to formally register the group as an association through the Investment Promotion Authority and Internal Revenue Commission so that they can access funding to send members to technical colleges to learn trade skills.

On a professional level, Kambi wants to become a seasoned banker in the country’s financial sector. He believes that financial literacy will break cycles of poverty and poor decision-making.

From being a young man shaped by the streets of Hohola, to a banker and father-of-two trying to shape the future of its young people, Kambi’s story proves that transformation is possible and, sometimes, the change a community needs is already existing in it.

He believes that helping others can change society in general.

 “Helping one person helps save the lives of a thousand generations that come from that one person.” His vision is simple but powerful: help youths and equip them with blue-collar skills so they can provide for their families with dignity.

 “I am tired of people complaining about the Government but have done nothing. This is my bit towards nation-building.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urban elites, rural exclusion and politics of growth
item.description: Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries. This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.
item.content:encoded:
Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries.

This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.

Although the article was published over a decade ago, its arguments are strikingly relevant today.

In the article, Krishna argues that the divide in today’s world is no longer only between countries but increasingly within countries.

Wealth, opportunity and political influence are concentrated through the process of urban agglomeration and the creation of urban elites while rural areas stagnate in the background.

Fast-growing economies such as China, India, Indonesia and South Africa now contain both those who are elite and those who are poor by global standards.

Krishna shows that poverty rates are consistently higher in rural areas.

For instance, in Malawi, 57 per cent of the rural population lives below the poverty line, compared with 17 per cent of urban residents.

In Zambia, the figures are 78 per cent rural versus 28 per cent urban; in Vietnam, it’s 22 per cent versus five per cent in rural and urban areas, respectively.

Over the decade these numbers might have changed, but still the argument made then holds equally for Papua New Guinea and its Melanesian neighbours, as World Bank data shows (Figure 1).

In Ethiopia, 97 per cent of urban residents have access to improved drinking water, compared to only 42 per cent of rural residents.

In India, 60 per cent of city residents have improved sanitation facilities versus only 25 per cent in rural areas.

In PNG, rural sanitation is at 15 per cent whereas urban sanitation rates hover at 49 per cent.

 Educational attainment is similarly unequal.

In Pakistan, 61 per cent of urban residents complete secondary education, compared with half of that figure for rural residents.

Even when years of schooling are similar, learning outcomes are poorer in rural schools.

Rural areas are poorer, but urban areas are more productive.

According to Krishna, this results in a governance dilemma: should governments prioritise poor rural households or globally competitive households in capital cities?

In theory, democratic systems should balance both.

Despite making up only a small proportion of the total population, urban elites’ preferences shape policy priorities, public investment decisions and national development narratives. 

Governments build airports, information superhighways and elite universities in urban centres, but rural schools, health clinics, roads and internet access are underfunded.

All this results, Krishna argues, in rural populations’ being locked into informal, insecure and sometimes sordid forms of work.

When economic mobility out of rural poverty is blocked, control of the state becomes the most reliable route to wealth.

I can’t help but think of my own country.

During the first general election in 1977, two years after independence, under 900 persons contested in PNG’s national elections.

The last general election in 2022 saw around 3,619 candidates contest the national election.

Even after accounting for population growth, competition for political power has intensified.

There were 31 contestants per 100,000 persons in 1977.

That number rose to 35.5 contestants per 100,000 persons in 2022 – an increase of about 15 per cent. 

Ask any Papua New Guinean about their future aspirations and chances are high they will say that they want to become a politician.
